professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Vertical farms — multi-story buildings where crops grow under LED lights in stacked trays — are spreading quickly in cities like Singapore and Seoul. Supporters say they cut transportation emissions, save water through closed-loop irrigation, and put fresh produce within walking distance of consumers. But critics point out that the lights and climate systems often run on fossil-fuel electricity, and the high startup cost limits who actually benefits. Do you think cities should invest public funds in vertical farms?

students.name: Diane
students.text: I live in a neighborhood where the closest grocery store is a 25-minute bus ride away, so I really like the idea of vertical farms. Last summer one opened on top of an old factory near my building, and they sell lettuce, basil, and strawberries for almost the same price as the supermarket. Even if the energy comes from natural gas right now, the system can switch to solar once panels are added. Public funding makes sense because the people who benefit most can't usually afford the high grocery delivery fees.

students.name: Marco
students.text: I see why a place like Diane's neighborhood would love them, but I'm not convinced cities should pay. The LEDs in those buildings draw a huge amount of power, and a recent report from my hometown showed one vertical farm used more electricity in a month than 200 apartments combined. That's a serious problem when most of the grid still burns gas. I'd rather see public money go toward rooftop greenhouses, which use real sunlight and cost much less to maintain. Vertical farms can stay private for now.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Today, we're going to talk about a small but growing trend in city government — paying residents small cash rewards, usually $5 to $20, for reporting infrastructure problems like potholes or broken streetlights through a mobile app. Cities that try this say repairs get done two to three times faster, but skeptics argue that the program turns neighbors into informants and unfairly rewards people with smartphones. Should city governments pay residents for these reports?

students.name: Claire
students.text: I think cash rewards are a smart use of public money. My uncle works for a city public works department and he says that until they launched their reporting app, they relied on monthly road surveys that missed half the damaged blocks. Now they fix potholes in about three days instead of three months. The rewards are tiny compared to what a broken-axle lawsuit costs the city, and $5 doesn't really turn anyone into a paid informant. Most reports come from people who'd file them anyway.

students.name: Paul
students.text: I'd be more comfortable if cities just hired more maintenance inspectors. The reward system has a quiet bias — older residents and people without good phones get left out, even though they often live near the worst infrastructure. Plus, what counts as 'reportable' starts to shift over time: in one program I read about, drivers started reporting parked cars they didn't like as 'obstructions.' Once you attach money to reporting your neighbors, the line gets messy.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Should every senior capstone project in a computer science program be released under an open-source license by default? Universities like Stanford and the University of Toronto already require this, arguing that students learn more from peer review and that society benefits when academic code is freely available. Other programs push back, noting that students who later try to commercialize their project face real difficulty raising investment if the code is already public. Which side do you think is right?

students.name: Yuki
students.text: I would have learned so much faster if our capstone code had been open by default. In my second year, I spent three weeks trying to write a tree-balancing routine that, it turned out, a senior project from 2021 had already solved beautifully — but the code was locked in a private repo that nobody at our school could read. Open-source review also forces students to write code other people can actually understand. Commercialization concerns are mostly overblown; very few capstones become real companies.

students.name: Cameron
students.text: I agree that openness is great in theory, but it should be the student's choice — not a default. My older sister did her capstone on a small ML tool that she later turned into a startup, and she told me their first investor specifically asked whether the source had been published. If it had been, the valuation would have dropped sharply. Schools could compromise by making projects open-source after two years, giving students a window to commercialize. That respects both learning and ownership.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: As we mentioned in class last week, museums around the world are split on whether to charge admission. London's British Museum is free to all visitors, supported by government funding, while New York's Metropolitan now charges $30 to out-of-state adults. Free entry removes a major barrier for low-income families and school groups, but critics point out that government grants can shrink during recessions, leaving 'free' museums dangerously underfunded. Should public museums charge entry fees?

students.name: Leah
students.text: I think museums should stay free, even if it means accepting some financial risk. My family couldn't afford regular trips to the local art museum when I was growing up, and the few free Sunday afternoons we got there changed the way I think about art and history. The Metropolitan's $30 fee effectively tells working families that culture is for someone else. If recessions threaten funding, cities can build small endowments or charge for special exhibits instead of everyday admission.

students.name: Ronald
students.text: I see Leah's point, but I think a modest fee actually protects the museum. The town I grew up in had a free local history museum that closed three years ago because the city had to cut its grant during the last recession. Now we have nothing. A $10 fee, with free admission for students and seniors, would have kept the lights on. Free should be a goal, not a fixed rule — and when free isn't sustainable, a small fee everyone pays is better than losing the institution entirely.
